title: Traveller's notebook leather cover
subtitle: Making a folded leather cover to hold multiple small insert notebooks
excerpt: A traveller's notebook cover is a single piece of soft leather, scored and folded, with an elastic cord threaded through the spine. Thin blank notebooks slip over the elastic and are held loosely inside. The cover gets better with use: the leather softens around the notebooks it carries. The construction takes a morning and produces a cover that lasts years.
sourceNotes: Traveller's notebook construction is synthesised from leatherwork and bookbinding practice. The format was popularised by the commercially produced Midori Traveler's Notebook and is widely reproduced in the hand-bookbinding and leather-craft community.

glossaryTerms.definition: The scored and folded strip of leather at the centre of the cover, where the elastic runs and the insert notebooks are held. The channel is formed by two parallel score lines about 10 mm apart.

content.text: The cover is cut from a single piece of veg-tan leather. The key measurement is the height of the insert notebooks: the cover height should match the insert height exactly (typically 210 mm for A5 inserts, or 120 mm for the pocket format). The cover width is twice the insert width plus the
, plus a small amount for the cover overhang.

attrs.title: Cutting leather safely
attrs.body: Use the craft knife with a fresh blade. A dull blade requires more pressure, which increases the risk of the knife slipping. Always cut against the metal ruler, never freehand. Keep fingers clear of the blade path.

items.name: Veg-tan leather, 1, 1.5 mm (slug: veg-tan-leather-thin)
items.description: A piece at least 250 × 230 mm for an A5-format cover. The leather should be soft enough to fold without cracking.

items.name: Round elastic cord, 3 mm (slug: round-elastic-cord)
items.description: For the spine and closure. A 600 mm length is enough for spine plus one closure loop.

items.name: Craft knife (slug: craft-knife)
items.description: For cutting the leather. Use a fresh No. 11 blade.

items.name: Cutting mat, A3 (slug: cutting-mat-a3)
items.description: The leather should lie fully flat on the mat during cutting.

items.name: Metal ruler, 30 cm (slug: metal-ruler-30cm)
items.description: For guided cuts and score lines.

items.name: Bookbinding awl (slug: bookbinding-awl)
items.description: For piercing the elastic holes at the head and tail of the spine.

items.name: Bone folder (slug: bone-folder)
items.description: For scoring and sharpening the spine fold line.

content.text: Cut the leather to: height = insert height + 10 mm overhang top and bottom. Width = (insert width × 2) + 10 mm spine channel + 15 mm total overhang. For standard A5 inserts (105 × 210 mm finished), the cover cuts to approximately 230 × 240 mm. Mark all measurements on the flesh side of the leather in pencil before cutting.

On the flesh side, mark two parallel lines 10 mm apart at the centre of the cover width. These form the spine channel. Score both lines firmly with the bone folder (not the knife): you are compressing and marking the fibre, not cutting it. Fold the cover along the spine channel to check that the fold is clean and the channel sits flat when closed. Dampen the spine area very slightly with water if the fold is too stiff.

Pierce a hole at the top centre and bottom centre of the spine channel with the bookbinding awl. The holes should be large enough to pass the elastic cord through with gentle pressure. Thread the elastic in from the outside: down through the top hole, across the inside of the channel, and out through the bottom hole. Tie a knot in each tail to prevent the cord pulling through. The span of elastic across the inside of the channel is where the insert notebook spines will rest.

Pierce a small hole at the centre of the right-hand edge of the cover. Thread a 200 mm length of elastic through and knot the tail on the inside. The loose end wraps around the closed cover and hooks over a button or a bone toggle attached to the spine, or simply wraps around the cover twice. No additional hardware is necessary.
